sections.section_title: These three appeals of different assessees of the same group are directed against separate but identical orders passed by the CIT(A)-I, jodhpur, dt. 28th Feb., 1995, relevant to asst. yr. 1989-90. Sin

sections.heading_hi: these three appeals का different assessees का the same group are directed के विरुद्ध separate but identical orders passed द्वारा the cit(a)-i, jodhpur, dt. 28th feb., 1995, relevant को asst. yr. 1989-90. sin
sections.full_text: These three appeals of different assessees of the same group are directed against separate but identical orders passed by the CIT(A)-I, jodhpur, dt. 28th Feb., 1995, relevant to asst. yr. 1989-90. Since all the appeals involve identical issues and similar facts and were heard together, therefore, are being disposed off by this single order for the, sake of convenience.

sections.section_title: Since there is no difference in the facts of these cases, so the facts in the case of Shri A.K. Modi are discussed, which indicate that the assessee had shown share income from M/s Modi Engineers. The
sections.heading_en: Since there is no difference in the facts of these cases, so the facts in the case of Shri A.K. Modi are discussed, which indicate that the assessee had shown share income from M/s Modi Engineers. The
sections.heading_hi: since there is no difference में the facts का these cases, so the facts में the case का shri a.k. modi are discussed, which indicate that the assessee had shown share income से m/s modi engineers. the
sections.full_text: Since there is no difference in the facts of these cases, so the facts in the case of Shri A.K. Modi are discussed, which indicate that the assessee had shown share income from M/s Modi Engineers. The assessee had also shown income from capital gains as also from salary. The assessee has further returned income from other sources. The assessment was made under Section 143(3) on 23rd March, 1990. Thereafter the matter went before the CIT(A) on the issue of standard deduction under Section 16(1). The learned CIT(A) vide his order dt. 3rd July, 1991 decided the issue in favour of the assessee. The effect to CIT(A)'s order was given on 9th Oct., 1991, Thereafter, the AO issued notice under Section 154/155 to rectify the assessment as according to him the income from other sources of the assessee had to be proportionately enhanced in view of the extended period of 21 months. The assessee objected to the AO's action for the reason that there was no underassessment or mistake for rectification because the income had been correctly shown and assessed. The assessee also raised the objection that the assessment order under Section 143(3) sought to be revised under Section 154 was passed beyond a period of 4 years back and therefore it could not be rectified. The AO however, rejected the contentions of the assessee and proceeded to rectify the assessment and enhanced the income from other sources of the assessee. The assessee has objected to this action of the AO for the reason that the AO had not given any basis as to how he came to the conclusion that the income of the assessee was not correctly assessed. It was thus, contended that nothing was clear from the AO's order as to how he had worked out the revised income of the assessee in the order appealed against because no details of working were given.

sections.section_title: The learned CIT(A), while considering the matter, agreed with the assessee. The AO's order is not clear and moreover, no statistical delta is given as to how the AO has come to the conclusion that the
sections.heading_en: The learned CIT(A), while considering the matter, agreed with the assessee. The AO's order is not clear and moreover, no statistical delta is given as to how the AO has come to the conclusion that the
sections.heading_hi: the learned cit(a), while considering the matter, agreed सहित the assessee. the ao's order is not clear और moreover, no statistical delta is given as को how the ao has come को the conclusion that the
sections.full_text: The learned CIT(A), while considering the matter, agreed with the assessee. The AO's order is not clear and moreover, no statistical delta is given as to how the AO has come to the conclusion that the income was correctly shown/assessed, in the order sought to be rectified. No working is given as to how the AO has arrived at the figure of Rs. 2,83,415. Only the AO has given some details of multiplication from which nothing can be inferred and the assessee has contended that he has shown correct income and the same was correctly assessed by the AO under Section 143(3). The AO is found to have not dealt this contention of the assessee and he has also not controverted the arguments of the assessee. So, this issue was restored to the file of the AO for re-examination and fresh orders after allowing the assessee to have his say with further stipulation that the AO shall deal with each and every argument taken by the assessee before coming to the specific finding. The learned CIT(A) further stated that the assessee has taken legal objection also that the original assessment order passed under Section 143(3) on 23rd March, 1990. The AO sought to rectify the same vide his notice dt. 13th Dec., 1994. According to the assessee, the notice issued by the AO for rectification was clearly beyond 4 years because the four years period expired on 31st March, 1994. It was further stated that these facts were available in the order of rectification passed by the AO and that there was no dispute about the dates. Hence, it was contended that the order appealed against is illegal and therefore, should be cancelled. The learned CIT(A) after consideration of the plea of the assessee has opined that he had already restored the matter of rectification for finding out the facts, so this legal objection of the assessee will also be dealt with by the AO at the time of re-examination as directed in the earlier part of the order. The AO shall squarely meet the objection of the assessee before corning to a specific conclusion. The assessee also urged before the CIT(A) that rectification application on the issue of standard deduction is also pending before the AO, so directed the AO to decide the same while giving effect to the order. The assessment was, therefore, set aside to be refrained.

sections.section_title: Aggrieved by this order of the learned CIT(A), the assessee is in further appeal and it is submitted that the CIT(A) has grossly erred in restoring back the legal issue relating to challenge to the ti
sections.heading_en: Aggrieved by this order of the learned CIT(A), the assessee is in further appeal and it is submitted that the CIT(A) has grossly erred in restoring back the legal issue relating to challenge to the ti
sections.heading_hi: aggrieved द्वारा this order का the learned cit(a), the assessee is में further appeal और it is submitted that the cit(a) has grossly erred में restoring back the legal issue relating को challenge को the ti
sections.full_text: Aggrieved by this order of the learned CIT(A), the assessee is in further appeal and it is submitted that the CIT(A) has grossly erred in restoring back the legal issue relating to challenge to the time barring action by the AO in passing order under Section 154 of the Act, which was the subject-matter of appeal before the learned CIT(A). When the learned AO has categorically held in the order passed under Section 154 that the time limit for rectification of the order was to be calculated from the date of the order giving effect to the order of the learned CIT(A), whereas in which order giving appeal effect, no such issue was involved before the CIT(A) in the appeal against the original order under Section 143(3). In view of the specific and absolute finding given by the AO in the impugned order under Section 154. The learned CIT(A) was thus in error in restoring this matter to the file of the AO, also amongst others, particularly inspite of the fact that he himself has arrived at the conclusion that the order under appeal before him was barred by limitation. Thus, the assessee prayed that the action of the CIT(A) should be cancelled by holding that the order of rectification challenged before the CIT(A) was clearly barred by limitation, therefore all actions are sustainable and invalid in the eyes of law.

sections.section_title: The learned CIT(A) has erroneously and without authority of law restored the rectification order and thereby allowing the AO a further time which is otherwise not available, therefore, the action of t
sections.heading_en: The learned CIT(A) has erroneously and without authority of law restored the rectification order and thereby allowing the AO a further time which is otherwise not available, therefore, the action of t
sections.heading_hi: the learned cit(a) has erroneously और बिना authority का law restored the rectification order और thereby allowing the ao a further time which is otherwise not available, therefore, the action का t
sections.full_text: The learned CIT(A) has erroneously and without authority of law restored the rectification order and thereby allowing the AO a further time which is otherwise not available, therefore, the action of the AO was wholly erroneous and against the provisions of law, and, therefore, he has erred in not allowing the appeal of the assessee. This issue is to be decided here, and without prejudice to the foregoing contentions/if the above grounds fails, when the entire factual positions and contentions supported by relevant documentary evidence were available before the CIT(A); he was under a legal obligation to decide the case on merit as well instead of restoring it back to the file of the. AO and if the AO's order lacked propriety, the learned CIT(A) ought to have deleted the variations made by the AO in the original assessed income as per material available before him. So, this Bench of the Tribunal is requested to decide the issue on merits. Since the first appeal was with relation to standard deduction, so the order of the AO with respect to salary income merged with the order of the first appellate authority and not the entire order. Reliance was placed on the decisions in Poonjabhai Vanmalidas v. WTO (1978) 114 ITR 38 (Guj) and CIT v. Sakseria Cotton Mills Ltd. (1980) 124 ITR 570 (Bom), so far as the merits of the case are concerned.

sections.section_title: The learned Departmental Representative relied on the order of the CIT(A) and pleaded that since the order of assessment is merged into the order of the CIT(A) in appeal filed against order under Sect
sections.heading_en: The learned Departmental Representative relied on the order of the CIT(A) and pleaded that since the order of assessment is merged into the order of the CIT(A) in appeal filed against order under Sect
sections.heading_hi: the learned departmental representative relied पर the order का the cit(a) और pleaded that since the order का assessment is merged into the order का the cit(a) में appeal filed के विरुद्ध order के तहत sect
sections.full_text: The learned Departmental Representative relied on the order of the CIT(A) and pleaded that since the order of assessment is merged into the order of the CIT(A) in appeal filed against order under Section 143(3), therefore, the order of rectification passed by the AO is within stipulated time. Since the matter has been restored to the file of the AO in February, 1995, he must have decided the issue by now. Therefore, no useful purpose would be served, if any interference is done with the said order. It was urged for the confirmation of the impugned order in all the three cases.

Since the matter has been restored to the file of the AO in February, 1995, he must have decided the issue by now. Therefore, no useful purpose would be served, if any interference is done with the said order. It was urged for the confirmation of the impugned order in all the three cases.

sections.section_title: After hearing both the sides and considering the material available on record as well as case laws cited, I find that when point of limitation was raised before the AO who dealt with the same by passi
sections.heading_en: After hearing both the sides and considering the material available on record as well as case laws cited, I find that when point of limitation was raised before the AO who dealt with the same by passi
sections.heading_hi: after hearing both the sides और considering the material available पर record as well as case laws cited, i find that when point का limitation was raised before the ao who dealt सहित the same द्वारा passi
sections.full_text: After hearing both the sides and considering the material available on record as well as case laws cited, I find that when point of limitation was raised before the AO who dealt with the same by passing a specific order in this regard at the time of passing order an application of the assessee moved under Section 154, there was no justification for the learned CIT(A) to set aside the issue on his file for redeciding it. It was incumbent upon him to take up the issue himself and decide the same. Since he has not decided the issue and the matter is pending for quite long time, so I find it appropriate to decide the appeal here only. It is an undisputed fact that assessment order in all these cases under Section 143(3) were made on 23rd March, 1990 and assessee went in appeal and agitated the disallowance of standard deduction which was allowed by the CIT(A). But so far as the issue involved for rectification is concerned, those were not related to the appeal filed by the assessee or decision given by the appellate authority, therefore, assessment order in relation to issue relatable to rectification cannot be said to have merged with the order of the CIT(A). As such, time-limit for rectification of the assessment order would be relatable to the date of the order in consideration of date i.e., 23rd March, 1990 and not relatable to the appeal effect order passed on 9th Oct., 1991, as held by the AO. Since the rectification notice was issued beyond time-limit prescribed under Section 154, therefore, any order passed thereafter cannot be held to be a valid order. Since there is no dispute regarding the dates of passing assessment order and the date of issue of notice under Sections 154-155 by the AO which is beyond the four years time period. Therefore, I am of the considered view that the AO could not pass any order after 31st March, 1994. As such, the order passed by the AO under Section 154 in all these three cases is quashed, being barred by time-limitation prescribed. Since the order is being quashed on legal issue of limitation, therefore, other grounds raised on merits are not being adjudicated upon.
